speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Schweitzer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Continue with the story with the potatoes and the capo.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he had me. So we made-- we had little cookers. We made potatoes for ourselves. We got food, but it was nothing much. But as you know, every morning, 3:30 appell. You have the-- what's it called-- the [NON-ENGLISH] at 3 o'clock. Appel, aufstehen, raus! Everybody out, appell! What was going on in that barrack, people died. People were coughing. People had diarrhea. So appell! So we came out, appell.

speaker: interviewee
text: So everybody had to know their number, because when they were appell, when you get out of the barrack, the blockade is these. As a matter of fact, when you see these barracks, in the front when you got in, the [NON-ENGLISH], they had his own room made up with things where he lived with his own bed. He was already a better citizens. So coming out, they teached us like in the army. [GERMAN]. Old people standing next to us, they were dying already. So they-- 30 degrees under zero early in the morning. When the capo counted us, [SPEAKING GERMAN], when the unterscharfuhrer came, then the capo had to put this out together and say, here is 70 people in this barrack. The unterscharfuhrer [SPEAKING GERMAN]. So many people, and then the unterscharfuhrer started also counting. But every day, there was four or five people falling dead at the appell. In the beginning, of course, we were not used to it. Then they were training us after the appell was finished, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? That always happened with the SS, when they saw you. So you had to-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]. What? Poof! One in the face. Until you knew [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Please show me.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the number. [GERMAN]. That was your name. If any SS man spoke to you, that you had to answer. We learnt it quick. But the old people didn't. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Life went on. You got used to it. I met a little loifer there. I played for the band. I sang. I went into these barracks. I saw what went on. It's a pity the [GERMAN] guards there. But then only with them. I didn't understand it. I didn't want to know either.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened, day per day, I saw the [NON-ENGLISH] there, Erich. I remember his first name. He had a nice uniform. And I said, Erich, can't you get me also a job like this? I liked that he had boots, and he had a [INAUDIBLE] coat. Nicely dressed, because we had tailor shops in there. And so he said, I will try it. So I played with the band. I walked about. Every day I looked at the stars. Like I told you, I saw what went on there. Every day the barracks, in the morning when you got up, you came out. There was always like 20, 30 bodies in front of the barracks, dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the whole commando that was really the orchestra who played, they had to do that. Pull it and put the dead bodies on there. But they were first always counted by appell, was always-- and these bodies was yellow and green from colors. But that was a normal-- normal day-- normal day in the Birkenau camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: If they died in the barracks, who brought them out?

speaker: interviewee
text: The what's it called, the-- the people themselves. Some maybe said kaddish. There was many times said kaddish for these people in privacy. And they were strang back onto the end of the camp. They were assembled there, counted up, and on the [INAUDIBLE] into the gas chamber. They were burned then. Normal what's it called, the normal--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you know that the burnings started? When did you know when you got to the camp that this--

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, Erich, the [GERMAN], Erich, suddenly the laufer, there was a selection. I was there three, four months. There was already selection after three, four months. [INAUDIBLE] I don't know the exact date, because nobody kept dates. That was in '44. And his family had to go into the gas chambers. And he could have stayed. There was a small group went in behind. The SS said, you don't have to go. And Erich said, I'm not leaving my grandparents and my-- and my mother and father. I don't leave them. Coming back, if my grandparents wouldn't have died a natural death, which they did, that I can say to God thanks that He took that former mensch, mine Opa, that He took him to Him in that dying way, instead of that man had to march into the gas chamber. And I would have gone with them. I was given the way-- a free way, but my grandmother ask me. This is my imagination. That's why I'm here today. Otherwise, I would have gone with them like Erich went with them. Erich went with their parents. And the job, I remember when they went, it was in the evening, a very cold afternoon, evening. And I said it also to Serge, that's why Serge said-- said the Hatikvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That transport that's known from [INAUDIBLE] to [? B lager ?], they sang the Hatikvah in the gas chamber, and we heard it. We heard it, because that camp was not far from the crematoriums. And it was so cold. And it was so lonely. You could hear a pin fall, you know? And we hear them sing the Hatikvah. When did you know that Birkenau wasn't just a play-- a lager, but it was really a death camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: After this, being a laufer--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: And running about to the gate--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and on the gate. All my friends know it, from the group. I saw everything. And-- and also, you saw the burning, the flames.

speaker: interviewer
text: So only--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the smell. But I don't understand. I didn't take notice of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to live day-for-day. You didn't take notice. You-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: This life of mine, as a little child, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was like a flower. Every day you were alive, then you got a bit of water, and you can go again. And so-- so we didn't think about it. We saw, we smelled. But if you-- if you stayed there the whole time looking at that, then you can't-- you can't exist. You can't survive. So what is it? We-- we passed it like we didn't-- we didn't see it. Did you pretend you didn't see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my underconscience, you know? Definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. I knew. Also when they-- I was on the gate when the commandos walked out to work at 6 o'clock in the morning. And we had to play. The orchestra played, and I had to say links, rechts, links.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to myself, and I saw these people passing there, 50, 60 people. What, are they like an army? They can hardly stand on their feet. And at nighttime they came back again. It was the same thing. They used those people until they-- they studied it. How long can a human being suffering like this hold out?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Mengele. Not Dr. Mengele. To me he's not a doctor. To me he was a [GERMAN], and I called him that. I never said-- some of them say doctor, even now [INAUDIBLE] think it's an insult. He-- he was not entitled to be a doctor. He was a monster.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he studied. He had a staff of Jewish people, Jewish-- and he studied. Diarrhea. They studied how long can you-- these people went out with commando to work. They had to build the crematoriums, they had to build the [GERMAN]. When they came back at night, each night they came back, they had less weight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were 30 kilos, they were Muselmann, then they didn't march out to the [INAUDIBLE] commando, they march into the gas chamber to the left. That we already start knowing. One day, a whole transport came in with all old women. You see, I was there. I don't know why I was there. I don't know. I feel like-- like a prophet or something. They took these women down from the-- from the vans, from the trucks, and they put them into barracks and [NON-ENGLISH], probably didn't have any place in the gas chamber. Sometimes they didn't have enough space, and they couldn't do the work they wanted to do, capacity.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these people, oh-- I went one evening-- because I was a laufer, I could do that. Other people couldn't. Into the barrack. I opened that door. At least they thought some of them were, oh, [? hashlah, ?] old people 75 aged. [NON-ENGLISH], where you been? Come. I went to them. Come, they said, come, come here. I went to them. I sat on their-- [INAUDIBLE] I gave them a few minutes thinking that I was their family. And they-- they felt good. And I went out and I said, oh, oh, what are they doing to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, that whole barrack was emptied into the gas chamber. The next day. So I saw all that. But you've got to remember I was a little child, 13 and 1/2, after my-- today I see really what it was. The realism, now I see. Unbelievable, because this Mengele, [GERMAN] Mengele.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was as a laufer there in July '44, every day I organized. I went sometimes on the ramps when the transports came in, the Hungarians, and I stole. I stole. I stole an-- unbelievable. I stole, and I felt awful. Oh yeah, [GERMAN] steals from deine menschen? I had that-- you know, a child feels more when they done something bad than a grown up.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't steal, because they didn't get it back. They had to-- they went into the gas chamber. And there was salami, Hungarian salami, sugar, and I organized. I put them up, and I was on the gate. And I knew the SS, because many people who came back were searched. If they found a potato or two, they were shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was-- and my mother, when I came into the barracks, I had sugar. And I wanted to unpack the sugar. There was a gold watch in it with a-- with a bracelet. Who wants the gold watch? Next day, Arpetski on the gate. Bellina, they called me, come here. Come. Where is that gold watch? Oh, yeah. Oh, somebody reported me. I had to hide it. I didn't want it, but if I would have told them, then he knew I stole the sugar.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had 25-- what do you call it-- on my back. You had to go over a little cushion with the oxen tail. 25. After the fourth hit, I was already out of what you call it. 21st, I had to lie three months on my stomach. I couldn't lie on my back, because somebody gave me-- but it want the--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was there, and I saw everything what happened. I saw the Canada commando, all these barracks. Now I know. Then I didn't-- I didn't realize it. I went in, because we had to leave everything on the ramp. And the SS used the women and the men to bring the stuff to the Canada commando, which were 10 or 12 barracks, and they had to sort everything out.

speaker: interviewee
text: One bag was full with watches. One bag was for the glasses. One bag for they were setting people, diamond experts. Diamond experts. They were checking the diamonds. That's why they didn't gas everybody. The people they could use, the SS, wouldn't go to the gas chamber. Because they had to have them for that reason. They didn't only hate the Jews because being Jews, but stole things off us. You would say the theft, stealing, like-- like gangsters, because that was also the reason. I don't think the Germans did it because they hated us so much. They knew that the-- that the Jews were quite-- let's say we were quite well-off. Good families. Doctors, jewelers, diamond dealers. They know all of that. In these barracks, I saw all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when you see today the shoes lying there that the-- the soles are open. This is because, I always thought to myself, why? Because they open it and the people have hidden things there as well. Like the sugar I found, and the watch I found in the sugar, they had sewed. So [INAUDIBLE] I went through that without even thinking, well, I said, yeah, tomorrow you [? will ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day you woke up, you went to the appell, and the appell was finished. You were a happy man. You went to your work. Sometimes appell was not finished. Sometimes you had to stay six hours, when somebody was running away. They kept you six hours standing. Many people fall dead. And when they found him, they found two people there. And they had to come back with the what's it called, [GERMAN]. With the paper. And then they-- they hit them to death. And then they ordered, in the hospital in the back, to make them alive again. It took them two months to bring them back to-- that-- that is the sadism. I don't understand that. And these people were brought back, looked after, and after the two months they were well, they were hanged. And everybody had to pass, and they said to us, If you do the same, that will happen to you. And they hanged them on the table. They pulled away the table without-- what's it called-- without the neck. They was-- and they were-- this was also horrible. That took 10 minutes, 15, they were hanging there. Unbelievable. And we had to look at that. That is everyday life. What happened there was something unhuman. Unbelievable. When some-- they caught a big-- three of them, and the three they caught, they brought them into the camp. They were dead already. They put them on the chair in front of the gate. The music marched-- the march music had to play. They had the bullet in here. It went there. And they were tied around the shirt. I sit here. And everybody in the camp had to pass and look at these three people who wanted to run away. Sadism in the highest. Germans in the highest sadism which I can imagine that people went along. But that sort of thing, today I think about it. Mengele came in July. When Mengele came into the camp and general with his Mercedes, white gloves he had on, he was afraid. He was afraid of infection. Don't think he touched us with his-- with his hands. When he came in, then we knew already. Then we knew. The angel of death.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we knew. Mengele, Mengele, Mengele. Like the telephone. Mengele's da, Mengele's there. Oy, what's going on? Mengele's there. We heard all about, oy, oh, what-- oh, what's going to happen? [GERMAN]. What's going to happen? So he went into the barrack. He came out with twins. Or he came out with other people who had to be-- what do you call it-- taken back to Auschwitz to-- to experiment. When he took them into the car, that car was closed, he drove out. Everybody went, [SIGHS].

speaker: interviewee
text: One more day or two more days, they didn't come for us. Because when he came, either it was selection, or it was for his experiments. The day came when he came in, July the 14th, there was a selection in the barrack. Left-- He sat in the middle. There was a table, and you had to pass. He looked at everything. He looked at your-- what's it called-- your underneath body, and you had to turn around. Links, rechts. Links, rechts. Links, rechts. I came out. We were standing there all. We had to undress naked, and we got dressed. And one was talking to the other, the Czechs. I didn't talk to the Czechs boys. I stepped aside. I was thinking. I was on my own. I was really living in the camp my own. Because I said, oh, yeah, they're going to the gas chamber. We're going to the gas chamber. It's finished now. I saw [GERMAN, [GERMAN]. They were standing in the middle of the camp with Mengele. They had their boots with their riding boots and trousers on. And I said, I have to go up to him. A Czech boy said to me, [NON-ENGLISH], he's going to shoot you. I said, he's going to shoot me, or I'm going to the gas chamber. At least I can try. I went up to him. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Don't let me into the gas chamber. I will work for you. I will clean your boots. I will clean your clothes. I will clean your car. I will clean your motorcycles. Mengele. Tall man. Black hair. Good-looking man. Baby face man. You wouldn't have thought he was a sadist like that. He looked at me. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. From Berlin. Ah, a Berliner. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I'm going to give you some work in the daylager. [GERMAN]. Turned around like they did. I played the same way they did it, being a Jew. And they turned around, went away, and the little boy said-- I saw him two years after. I haven't found him since. Two years after, I found him in London, after--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] he said do the same for me, Hellmuth. Do the same for me, bitte. Do. I said okay. I went up again. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. You want me to give him also life? [SPEAKING GERMAN]. You know what I'm going to do with you? I'm going to take two matches. Who draws the longest match will stay alive, and the shorter goes into the gas chamber. They were enjoying themselves, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was saying [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I took it. I took the longest one. [SIGHS] Danke, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. The boy-- Harry, that was his first name-- he was-- [INAUDIBLE]. It was me who went back. I don't know what I had in me. I was six feet tall, back again. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Give him-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] He must have though, what [GERMAN]. OK. I put them into the kitchen in the what's it called, in the D lager. And then he saved all these 90 boys. We were together in Czechoslovakia now, and we still ask. Some of the group came up to me and said, Hellmuth, thank you. The boy came up to me in London two years after. He came up to my house. I forgot the school life. I want to thank you. You saved my life. I didn't do this. I didn't save your life. Upstairs, he saved our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: But these things, psychologically they want to know now, why has Mengele saved 90 boys, 13, 14 years of age as well? There were 350 kids. The other ones went in the gas chamber, and us he has saved. But these things, psychologically, I think even now as well, being 65 years of age. I must've turned around his-- what's it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: If a Jewish boy like this, a little Jewish boy asks me to save the life of another, I can use these other 90 boys as well. Mengele was two personalities. He was sometimes very good. You could talk to him. And the other time he was a bastard. He was that Dr. Jekyll and Dr. Hyde. The famous-- afterward, because I studied it. I studied the whole SS when I was there, the sadism. You must have never shown unto the SS that you cried. You was never-- when they hit you, you have to get up and [SPEAKING GERMAN] until they stop hitting you. They said, how can you-- it was-- My name, later when I came in D lager, I was with two other boys, a Polish boy and a Russian boy on the gate. And that was [SPEAKING GERMAN] who was the commandant. I was his laufer then, when Mengele gave me that. I studied these people. First of all, I had to clean his boots. When I came in the morning, he came with his motorcycles. They all-- they all had motorcycles. Mengele had a-- what's it called-- he was [GERMAN]. He has his car, because he had to go to-- And they came, and then said, Berliner, putz this motorcycle. And Berliner-- so I had to come into his office in front of every barrack camp. There was a barrack for the SS that their paperwork and whatever they did, and they were eating there. And the first thing when he came there, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. [GERMAN]. Take off the boots. He was sitting there. From the back, I had to do it like this. I put them up and I cleaned. I cleaned that they-- like a mirror. But when he took the jacket off, he took his hat off, he put his hat on the-- what's it called. He was sitting there. That [GERMAN] was suddenly not 6 feet tall. He was 5 feet tall. I study that. He was nobody. Without that uniform, he was nobody. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And he had that uniform, and he closed it up to the top with that thing, and he was a different man. There was-- he was a different man. He was a teufel. He was a sadist. [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: So that is the life from my side which I studied. The day came-- the day came that one day, he came to gate. Because on that gate where I was, in D lager, they all met. Hess came there. [INAUDIBLE] came there. Mull came there from the gas chambers, the commandant. I knew them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Irma Grese came there. Irma Grese came. She said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. [GERMAN], she said to me. Come sit on my knee. You're a nice boy. Because I spoke German, being from Berlin. Well, I had to play that way, you know, because-- to survive. Like my mother said, to survive. So I played that way. I never hurt nobody. I played along with them, but I was never in their what's it called, [INAUDIBLE]. But I used them. I used them. You see, the Germans-- you saw the film with William Holden and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans in one way were stupid. They were schmucks in one way. So as-- as intelligent they were with their brains, there was always something missing there. There was something missing there. They did that work not alone, they did it with the [NON-ENGLISH]. But you could override them. And how could you override them? To be perfect, more perfect than they were. So I cleaned this motorcycle that clean. I went-- they had even-- It came so far they had even-- what's it called-- a little family. And I remember one day, he said, [GERMAN]-- he had these food in the camp. He had-- in the barrack he had a kitchen there. And they had a Ukraine, a [NON-ENGLISH] cook for him. He made for him French fries and all that. I also profited from that, you know? So he said, go to the farm and get me some eggs. So I took the Russian boy along, because we have to get eggs. And we came, and there was a little farmhouse like this room, and we saw the chickens sitting there and everything. So I said to the Russian, Come here. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Are you stupid? [SPEAKING GERMAN]. So tell me something, I said, can the chickens talk? I said, can the chickens talk? You schmuck, they can talk? But you know what happened? I came back. And the next day, [GERMAN] called me into his office. And he was going that Russian, yeah, Berliner, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? What did you do? Oh, yeah, [INAUDIBLE]. That bastard informed on me. But you know, there's a German word, I think in English maybe as well, he has it in [INAUDIBLE]. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I love somebody who reports you, but I hate the person who does it. And I said to [GERMAN], [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I don't say nothing. And [GERMAN]. I'm not an informer that way. But he respected that. That bastard respected that. He said, Berliner, if that will happen again one day, yeah, you know what's going to happen to you, eh? [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And I went to the Russian. I told him off, you know? So everyday life, the SS came there. They came with dogs. They stood there with 10 Alsatian dogs. When the boy [INAUDIBLE] commandos, of course when they came out, [? the ausser ?] commandos, they had to go along. The dogs had to go along with them. They couldn't run away with these-- with these long lines. And these dogs were trained. They hated every-- even me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were trained on the smell from the-- We had possibly that disinfection smell. It was trained onto them like dogs now can find drugs, probably. But they all came there, and they loved [INAUDIBLE]. And I was standing on [INAUDIBLE]. I was standing on the gate doing all the commissions. Sometimes I had to run to the [INAUDIBLE]. Sometimes I could have gone-- I went to the [GERMAN] lager outside. I saw the gypsies there, these little kids there. At [? four, ?] they played the violins. We sang. [GERMAN]. They were also--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? One day, [GERMAN], in the morning, he came there. He was standing on the-- on the gate, and he said, Berliner, I'm going to shoot you. On my life.
